results.title: Best Closely Alternative for 2026: Why Teams Pick Fuzzy | Fuzzy AI Blog
results.content:
The shift isn't complicated. Buyers in 2026 don't respond to strangers who show up with a pitch. They respond to people they recognise. People who've already shown up in their feed, engaged with their content, given them a reason to connect. Volume-first tools can't do that.

Fuzzy is the closely alternative built for teams that care about reply rates, not send rates. It combines AI-powered personalisation, authority-led sequences, and full conversation memory to turn cold outreach into warm conversations. Here's how it compares, and why teams are making the switch.

## What is Closely and where does it fall short? [...] ## Closely alternatives compared: feature breakdown

| Feature | Fuzzy AI | Closely | Expandi | HeyReach | Dripify | Waalaxy | MeetAlfred | Dux-Soup |
 ---  ---  ---  --- 
| Cloud-based | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| AI personalisation | 75+ vars | Basic | Basic | Basic | Basic | Basic | Basic | None |
| Authority warming | Yes | No | No | No | No | No | No | No |
| Context/memory | Yes | No | No | No | No | No | No | No |
| Multi-channel | LinkedIn+email | LinkedIn+email | LinkedIn+email | LinkedIn | LinkedIn | LinkedIn+email | Multi | LinkedIn |
| Team features | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Limited | Limited | Limited | No |
| Starting price |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| Mid-tier | ~$99/mo | ~$79/mo | ~$39/mo | Free tier | ~$59/mo | ~$11/mo |  |  |

Feature

Fuzzy AI [...] Thinner on AI.

Best for: Small teams wanting simple automation.

### 5. Waalaxy, best for solo operators

Waalaxy has a free tier and easy UX.

Limited AI, weak team features.

Best for: Solopreneurs.

### 6. MeetAlfred, best for multi-channel

MeetAlfred offers LinkedIn, email, and Twitter in one tool.

Jack of all trades, thin on AI.

Best for: Teams wanting basic multi-channel.

### 7. Dux-Soup, best for budget users

Dux-Soup is the classic Chrome extension. Cheap, familiar.

Browser-based, no AI.

Best for: Individual reps on a budget.

## Closely alternatives compared: feature breakdown

results.title: Fuzzy Matching or Other Alternativies?
results.content:
- What should I do to handle this? should I use fuzzy matching or is there any other tool that is the best to handle this? Fuzzy matching should work well if you match against a list of valid city names. If you can't use a list of valid city names, then "ChatGPT" may be a good solution. You can also ask ChatGPT to give a "confidence" score for each correction. You could also try smaller AI models such as DistilBERT. Fuzzy matching against a large list of city names is probably the best solution if you want a solution to run locally with limited computational resources. [...] - You can try interzoid...It's AI-based enhanced by LLM usage, uses similarity keys from APIs, enabling you to either choose or algorithmically select a preferred standard, and identify all similar permutations of a given data value that exists to then standardize. It goes way beyond the various string matching and distance algorithms. [...] ## Top Comments
- One approach you can try is fuzzy matching/identity resolution on your data and then linking against a known dataset of cities.
- There are several options you have with this type of data, you can apply fuzzy matching to the variations, you can tokenize and normalize the variations with a dictionary, and there is also the use of regular expressions in some cases. If you would like a no-code solution, you can PM me and I would be happy to help.
